B. Each head has its own learned linear projections for Q, K, V; head outputs are concatenated and then projected with a final linear layer
C. Heads are computed by running attention multiple times with different dropout seeds and picking the best head
D. Heads split the batch dimension so each head sees different training examples
questions.explanation: Standard multi-head attention uses separate learned projection matrices per head (often implemented as one big projection then reshaped). Each head performs attention independently, producing (B, L, d_k). The heads are concatenated to (B, L, h*d_k = d_model) and then passed through an output projection W_O. A, C, and D do not reflect how multi-head attention is implemented.

questions.question_text: Why is the dot-product attention score divided by sqrt(d_k) in scaled dot-product attention?
options: A. To make attention O(n) instead of O(n^2) in sequence length
B. To reduce the variance of QK^T as d_k grows, preventing softmax from saturating and improving gradient flow
C. To ensure Q and K are orthogonal so attention becomes sparse
D. To enforce that attention weights sum to 1 across the batch dimension
questions.explanation: If Q and K components are roughly zero-mean with unit variance, the dot product magnitude grows with d_k, making logits large and softmax peaky/saturated, which harms gradients. Dividing by sqrt(d_k) keeps logits in a more stable range. It does not change asymptotic complexity (A), does not force orthogonality (C), and softmax normalization is across the key dimension per query, not across the batch (D).

questions.question_text: In decoder self-attention for autoregressive generation, why do we apply a causal mask, and what would break if we forgot it during training?
questions.correct_answer: A causal (look-ahead) mask prevents each position t in the decoder from attending to future tokens > t. Without it during training, the model can “cheat” by using ground-truth future tokens to predict the current token, leading to unrealistically low training loss but poor generation at inference time (exposure to information it won’t have). It also changes the learned conditional distribution from p(y_t | y_<t, x) to something closer to p(y_t | y_<=T, x), which is not the desired autoregressive factorization.
questions.explanation: Decoder self-attention must respect the autoregressive constraint. The causal mask is typically an upper-triangular -inf added before softmax so attention weights to future positions become ~0. Forgetting it yields label leakage: training uses future context, but inference cannot, causing a train–test mismatch and degraded generation quality.

questions.question_text:
Scaled dot-product attention is:

Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax((QK^T)/sqrt(d_k) + M) V

Assume batch size B=2, sequence length L=128, model dim d_model=512, number of heads h=8, so per-head dim d_k = 64. In a standard implementation (per head), what are the shapes of Q, K, V and the attention weights matrix?

(Use shapes with batch and head dimensions.)
options: A. Q,K,V: (B, L, d_model); weights: (B, L, L)
B. Q,K,V: (B, h, L, d_k); weights: (B, h, L, L)
C. Q,K,V: (B, h, d_k, L); weights: (B, h, d_k, d_k)
D. Q,K,V: (B, L, h, d_k); weights: (B, L, h, L)
questions.explanation: After projecting to heads, Q/K/V are typically reshaped to (B, h, L, d_k). The attention scores are QK^T, producing (B, h, L, L) (each query position attends over all key positions). Option A ignores heads; C has transposed/incorrect axes and wrong weights shape; D mixes axes and yields an unusual weights layout.

questions.question_text:
You are choosing an architecture for two tasks:
1) Sentiment classification of a sentence
2) Machine translation (seq2seq)

Which mapping of Transformer components is most appropriate, and why?
options: A. (1) Decoder-only, (2) Encoder-only — because translation only needs bidirectional context
B. (1) Encoder-only, (2) Encoder-decoder — because classification needs a pooled representation, while translation needs cross-attention from target to source
C. (1) Encoder-decoder, (2) Decoder-only — because classification requires autoregressive generation
D. (1) CNN, (2) RNN — because Transformers cannot handle variable-length sequences
questions.explanation: Classification commonly uses an encoder (bidirectional self-attention) to produce contextual token representations that can be pooled (e.g., [CLS] token or mean pooling). Translation typically uses an encoder-decoder: the encoder processes the source sequence; the decoder generates the target autoregressively, using self-attention over previously generated tokens plus cross-attention to the encoder outputs. A/C/D are mismatched to typical practical designs.

questions.question_text: In a production NLP system you need to model long-range dependencies (e.g., coreference across long documents) and train efficiently on GPUs. Which is the best practical reason Transformers often replace RNNs for this use case?
options: A. Transformers are strictly more sample-efficient than RNNs on all sequence tasks
B. Self-attention enables parallel computation across tokens and provides shorter gradient paths than recurrent steps
C. Transformers require less memory because attention is O(n) in sequence length
D. Transformers do not need any form of normalization or residual connections to train stably
questions.explanation: Transformers avoid sequential recurrence: attention over all tokens can be computed in parallel on GPUs/TPUs, improving throughput. Also, information can flow between distant tokens in a single attention step (shorter effective path) rather than through many recurrent timesteps, helping optimization for long-range dependencies. Attention is typically O(n^2) in sequence length, so C is false; A and D are overgeneralizations/incorrect.
